title.value: An Overview of Diagnostic Captioning: Methods, Datasets, and Evaluation Measures
title.evidence: This article is an extensive overview of DC.

authors.evidence: John Pavlopoulos1,* Vasiliki Kougia1,2, Ion Androutsopoulos2, and Dimitris Papamichail3
authors.notes: Authors are affiliated with institutions in Sweden and Greece.

year.evidence: The paper does not explicitly state the publication year but is assumed to be current.
year.notes: Assumed from context as no explicit year is provided.

abstract.value: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination. DC can assist inexperienced physicians, reducing clinical errors. It can also help experienced physicians produce diagnostic reports faster. Following the advances of deep learning, especially in generic image captioning, DC has recently attracted more attention, leading to several systems and datasets. This article is an extensive overview of DC. It presents relevant datasets, evaluation measures, and up to date systems. It also highlights shortcomings that hinder DC’s progress and proposes future directions.
abstract.evidence: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination.

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

venue.notes: Assumed based on context; not explicitly mentioned.

problem_statement.value: The increasing workload on radiologists due to a rise in medical imaging examinations leads to a higher likelihood of clinical errors, necessitating tools that assist in producing accurate diagnostic reports efficiently.
problem_statement.evidence: An increased workload in this demanding task increases the likelihood of medical errors.

method_type.evidence: DC methods usually employ the encoder-decoder architecture... ignoring retrieval-based approaches.
method_type.notes: The paper discusses both deep learning and retrieval-based methods.

architecture.evidence: The most common system architecture in DC is the encoder-decoder approach.
architecture.notes: Encoder-decoder is the prevalent architecture in DC methods.

data_sources.evidence: we focus mostly on studying and discussing the characteristics of the remaining two datasets, namely IU X-RAY and MIMIC-CXR.
data_sources.notes: These datasets are pivotal for evaluating DC methods.

experiment_design.value: The experiments involved analyzing available datasets and evaluating different DC systems based on their performance metrics.
experiment_design.evidence: We provide a segmentation of current DC systems by the kinds of methods they use, also reporting evaluation scores for each system with all available measures.
experiment_design.notes: Focus on performance metrics and system segmentation.

evaluation_metrics.evidence: We discussed the shortcomings of current automatic evaluation DC measures.
evaluation_metrics.notes: Metrics often fail to capture clinical correctness.

value: Generated captions often fail to accurately reflect clinical conditions.
High lexical overlap does not guarantee clinical correctness.
qualitative_results.evidence: Current methods for automatically measuring clinical correctness produce results of poor quality.
qualitative_results.notes: Qualitative results highlight the gap between lexical accuracy and clinical correctness.

value: Current evaluation measures often fail to capture clinical correctness.
Datasets used for training are limited in size and diversity.
limitations.evidence: Evaluation measures employed by previous DC research mainly assess lexical overlap without directly assessing clinical correctness.
limitations.notes: Limitations emphasize the need for better evaluation metrics.

value: Develop hybrid methods combining encoder-decoder and retrieval-based approaches.
Improve evaluation measures to better assess clinical correctness.
future_work.evidence: Our main findings also guide our proposals for future work on DC.

provenance.section_header: 5 Conclusions and Directions for Future Research

future_work.notes: Future work should focus on hybrid approaches and evaluation improvements.

dataset_links.evidence: We focus mostly on studying and discussing the characteristics of the remaining two datasets, namely IU X-RAY and MIMIC-CXR.
dataset_links.notes: Links provided for the two main datasets discussed.
